title: Steam locomotive

infobox.value: is a type of [locomotive](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") that provides the force to move itself and other vehicles by means of the expansion of [steam](/wiki/Steam "Steam").[[1]](#cite_note-1): 80 It is fuelled by burning combustible material (usually [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") or, rarely, [wood](/wiki/Wood_fuel "Wood fuel")) to heat water in the locomotive's [boiler](/wiki/Boiler_(power_generation) "Boiler (power generation)") to the point where it becomes gaseous and its volume increases 1,600 to 1,700 times.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Functionally, it is a self-propelled [steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine") on wheels.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

[LNER Class A4 4468 *Mallard*](/wiki/LNER_Class_A4_4468_Mallard "LNER Class A4 4468 Mallard") is officially the fastest steam locomotive, reaching 126 mph (203 km/h) on 3 July 1938.

[LNER Class A3 4472 *Flying Scotsman*](/wiki/LNER_Class_A3_4472_Flying_Scotsman "LNER Class A3 4472 Flying Scotsman") was the first steam locomotive to officially reach 100 mph (160 km/h), on 30 November 1934.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/UP_4014_Departs_Denver%2C_CO_At_York_Street.jpg/250px-UP_4014_Departs_Denver%2C_CO_At_York_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:UP_4014_Departs_Denver,_CO_At_York_Street.jpg)

[Union Pacific *Big Boy* 4014](/wiki/Union_Pacific_4014 "Union Pacific 4014") is the largest operating steam locomotive in the world.



[41 018](/wiki/DRB_Class_41 "DRB Class 41") climbing the [Schiefe Ebene](/wiki/Schiefe_Ebene "Schiefe Ebene") with [01 1066](/wiki/DRG_Class_01.10 "DRG Class 01.10") as [pusher locomotive](/wiki/Bank_engine "Bank engine") (video 34.4 MB)

A **steam locomotive** is a type of [locomotive](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") that provides the force to move itself and other vehicles by means of the expansion of [steam](/wiki/Steam "Steam").[[1]](#cite_note-1): 80 It is fuelled by burning combustible material (usually [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") or, rarely, [wood](/wiki/Wood_fuel "Wood fuel")) to heat water in the locomotive's [boiler](/wiki/Boiler_(power_generation) "Boiler (power generation)") to the point where it becomes gaseous and its volume increases 1,600 to 1,700 times.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Functionally, it is a self-propelled [steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine") on wheels.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In most locomotives the steam is admitted alternately to each end of its [cylinders](/wiki/Steam_locomotive_components "Steam locomotive components") in which [pistons](/wiki/Piston "Piston") are mechanically connected to the locomotive's main wheels. Fuel and water supplies are usually carried with the locomotive, either on the locomotive itself or in a [tender](/wiki/Tender_(rail) "Tender (rail)") coupled to it. [Variations](#Variations) in this general design include electrically powered boilers, turbines in place of pistons, and using steam generated externally.

Steam locomotives were first developed in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") during the early 19th century and used for railway transport until the end of the 20th century. [Richard Trevithick](/wiki/Richard_Trevithick "Richard Trevithick") built the first steam locomotive known to have hauled a load over a distance at [Pen-y-darren](/wiki/Penydarren "Penydarren") in 1804, although he produced an earlier locomotive for trial at [Coalbrookdale](/wiki/Coalbrookdale "Coalbrookdale") in 1802.[[4]](#cite_note-4) *[Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(locomotive) "Salamanca (locomotive)")*, built in 1812 by [Matthew Murray](/wiki/Matthew_Murray "Matthew Murray") for the [Middleton Railway](/wiki/Middleton_Railway "Middleton Railway"), was the first commercially successful steam locomotive.[[5]](#cite_note-Hamilton_Ellis_1968_20-5) [*Locomotion* No. 1](/wiki/Locomotion_No._1 "Locomotion No. 1"), built by [George Stephenson](/wiki/George_Stephenson "George Stephenson") and his son [Robert's](/wiki/Robert_Stephenson "Robert Stephenson") company [Robert Stephenson and Company](/wiki/Robert_Stephenson_and_Company "Robert Stephenson and Company"), was the first steam locomotive to haul passengers on a public railway, the [Stockton and Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_and_Darlington_Railway "Stockton and Darlington Railway"), in 1825. Rapid development ensued; in 1830 George Stephenson opened the first public inter-city railway, the [Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool and Manchester Railway"), after the success of [Rocket](/wiki/Stephenson%27s_Rocket "Stephenson's Rocket") at the 1829 [Rainhill Trials](/wiki/Rainhill_Trials "Rainhill Trials") had proved that steam locomotives could perform such duties. Robert Stephenson and Company was the pre-eminent builder of steam locomotives in the first decades of steam for railways in the United Kingdom, the United States, and much of Europe.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Towards the end of the steam era, a longstanding British emphasis on speed culminated in a record, still unbroken, of 126 miles per hour (203 kilometres per hour) by [LNER Class A4 4468 *Mallard*](/wiki/LNER_Class_A4_4468_Mallard "LNER Class A4 4468 Mallard");[[7]](#cite_note-7) however, there are long-standing claims that the [Pennsylvania Railroad class S1](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad_class_S1 "Pennsylvania Railroad class S1") achieved speeds upwards of 150 mph, though this was never officially proven.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) In the United States, larger [loading gauges](/wiki/Loading_gauge#Passenger_service "Loading gauge") allowed the development of very large, heavy locomotives such as the [Union Pacific Big Boy](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Big_Boy "Union Pacific Big Boy"), which weighs 540 [long tons](/wiki/Long_ton "Long ton") (550 [t](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne"); 600 [short tons](/wiki/Short_ton "Short ton")) and has a tractive effort of 135,375 pounds-force (602,180 newtons).[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[note 1]](#cite_note-13)

Beginning in the early 1900s, steam locomotives were gradually superseded by [electric](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive") and [diesel locomotives](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive"), with railways fully converting to electric and diesel power beginning in the late 1930s. The majority of steam locomotives were retired from regular service by the 1980s, although several continue to run on tourist and heritage lines.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of rail transport](/wiki/History_of_rail_transport "History of rail transport") and [Category:Early steam locomotives](/wiki/Category:Early_steam_locomotives "Category:Early steam locomotives")

### Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Britain")]

The earliest railways employed horses to draw carts along [rail tracks](/wiki/Rail_tracks "Rail tracks").[[14]](#cite_note-15) In 1784, [William Murdoch](/wiki/William_Murdoch "William Murdoch"), a [Scottish](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") inventor, built a small-scale prototype of a steam road locomotive in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham").[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) A full-scale rail steam locomotive was proposed by [William Reynolds](/wiki/William_Reynolds_(industrialist) "William Reynolds (industrialist)") around 1787.[[17]](#cite_note-18) An early working model of a steam rail locomotive was designed and constructed by steamboat pioneer [John Fitch](/wiki/John_Fitch_(inventor) "John Fitch (inventor)") in the US during 1794.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Some sources claim Fitch's model was operable already by the 1780s and that he demonstrated his locomotive to [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington").[[19]](#cite_note-20) His steam locomotive used interior bladed wheels guided by rails or tracks. The model still exists at the [Ohio Historical Society](/wiki/Ohio_Historical_Society "Ohio Historical Society") Museum in Columbus, US.[[20]](#cite_note-21) The authenticity and date of this locomotive is disputed by some experts and a workable steam train would have to await the invention of the [high-pressure steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine#High-pressure_engines "Steam engine") by [Richard Trevithick](/wiki/Richard_Trevithick "Richard Trevithick"), who pioneered the use of steam locomotives.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Locomotive_trevithick.svg/250px-Locomotive_trevithick.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Locomotive_trevithick.svg)

Trevithick's 1802 Coalbrookdale locomotive

The first full-scale working railway steam locomotive was the 3 ft (914 mm) gauge *Coalbrookdale Locomotive* built by Trevithick in 1802. It was constructed for the [Coalbrookdale](/wiki/Coalbrookdale "Coalbrookdale") ironworks in [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") though no record of it working there has survived.[[22]](#cite_note-23) On 21 February 1804, the first recorded steam-hauled railway journey took place as another of Trevithick's locomotives hauled a train along the 4 ft 4 in (1,321 mm)-wide [tramway](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") from the [Pen-y-darren](/wiki/Pen-y-darren "Pen-y-darren") ironworks, near [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil "Merthyr Tydfil"), to [Abercynon](/wiki/Abercynon "Abercynon") in South Wales.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) Accompanied by [Andrew Vivian](/wiki/Andrew_Vivian "Andrew Vivian"), it ran with mixed success.[[25]](#cite_note-ODNBTrevithick-26) The design incorporated a number of important innovations that included using high-pressure steam which reduced the weight of the engine and increased its efficiency.

Trevithick visited the [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne") area in 1804 and had a ready audience of colliery (coal mine) owners and engineers. The visit was so successful that the colliery railways in north-east England became the leading centre for experimentation and development of the steam locomotive.[[26]](#cite_note-Garnett,_2005-27) Trevithick continued his own steam propulsion experiments through another trio of locomotives, concluding with the *[Catch Me Who Can](/wiki/Catch_Me_Who_Can "Catch Me Who Can")* in 1808, first in the world to haul fare-paying passengers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Blenkinsop%27s_rack_locomotive%2C_1812_%28British_Railway_Locomotives_1803-1853%29.jpg/250px-Blenkinsop%27s_rack_locomotive%2C_1812_%28British_Railway_Locomotives_1803-1853%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blenkinsop%27s_rack_locomotive,_1812_(British_Railway_Locomotives_1803-1853).jpg)

The *[Salamanca](/wiki/The_Salamanca "The Salamanca")* locomotive

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Locomotion_No.1%2C_Darlington.jpg/250px-Locomotion_No.1%2C_Darlington.jpg)](/wiki/File:Locomotion_No.1,_Darlington.jpg)

The *Locomotion* at [Darlington Railway Centre and Museum](/wiki/Head_of_Steam "Head of Steam")

In 1812, [Matthew Murray](/wiki/Matthew_Murray "Matthew Murray")'s successful twin-cylinder [rack](/wiki/Rack_railway "Rack railway") locomotive *[Salamanca](/wiki/The_Salamanca "The Salamanca")* first ran on the [edge-railed](/wiki/Wagonway#Edgeway,_edge_rails "Wagonway") [rack-and-pinion](/wiki/Rack_and_pinion "Rack and pinion") [Middleton Railway](/wiki/Middleton_Railway "Middleton Railway").[[27]](#cite_note-Young,1923-28) Another well-known early locomotive was *[Puffing Billy](/wiki/Puffing_Billy_(locomotive) "Puffing Billy (locomotive)")*, built 1813–14 by engineer [William Hedley](/wiki/William_Hedley "William Hedley"). It was intended to work on the Wylam Colliery near Newcastle upon Tyne. This locomotive is the oldest preserved, and is on static display at the [Science Museum, London](/wiki/Science_Museum,_London "Science Museum, London").

#### George Stephenson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: George Stephenson")]

[George Stephenson](/wiki/George_Stephenson "George Stephenson"), a former miner working as an engine-wright at [Killingworth Colliery](/wiki/Killingworth#Killingworth_Colliery "Killingworth"), developed up to sixteen [Killingworth locomotives](/wiki/Killingworth_locomotives "Killingworth locomotives"), including *[Blücher](/wiki/Bl%C3%BCcher_(locomotive) "Blücher (locomotive)")* in 1814, another in 1815, and a (newly identified) *Killingworth Billy* in 1816. He also constructed *The Duke* in 1817 for the [Kilmarnock and Troon Railway](/wiki/Kilmarnock_and_Troon_Railway "Kilmarnock and Troon Railway"), which was the first steam locomotive to work in Scotland.

In 1825, Stephenson built [Locomotion No. 1](/wiki/Locomotion_No._1 "Locomotion No. 1") for the [Stockton and Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_and_Darlington_Railway "Stockton and Darlington Railway"), north-east England, which was the first public steam railway in the world. In 1829, his son [Robert](/wiki/Robert_Stephenson "Robert Stephenson") built in Newcastle *[The Rocket](/wiki/The_Rocket_(locomotive) "The Rocket (locomotive)")*, which was entered in and won the [Rainhill Trials](/wiki/Rainhill_Trials "Rainhill Trials"). This success led to the company emerging as the pre-eminent builder of steam locomotives used on railways in the UK, US and much of Europe.[[28]](#cite_note-Ellis,1968-29) The [Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool and Manchester Railway") opened a year later making exclusive use of steam power for passenger and [goods trains](/wiki/Freight_train "Freight train").

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/The_Stourbridge_Lion.JPG/250px-The_Stourbridge_Lion.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Stourbridge_Lion.JPG)

The *Stourbridge Lion*

Before the arrival of British imports, some domestic steam locomotive prototypes were built and tested in the United States, including John Fitch's miniature prototype. A prominent full sized example was [Col. John Steven's](/wiki/John_Stevens_(inventor,_born_1749) "John Stevens (inventor, born 1749)") "steam wagon" which was demonstrated on a loop of track in [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey") in 1825.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Many of the earliest locomotives for commercial use on American railroads were imported from Great Britain, including first the *[Stourbridge Lion](/wiki/Stourbridge_Lion "Stourbridge Lion")* and later the *[John Bull](/wiki/John_Bull_(locomotive) "John Bull (locomotive)")*. However, a domestic locomotive-manufacturing industry was soon established. In 1830, the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad")'s *[Tom Thumb](/wiki/Tom_Thumb_(locomotive) "Tom Thumb (locomotive)")*, designed by [Peter Cooper](/wiki/Peter_Cooper "Peter Cooper"),[[30]](#cite_note-31) was the first commercial US-built locomotive to run in America; it was intended as a demonstration of the potential of steam traction rather than as a revenue-earning locomotive. The *[DeWitt Clinton](/wiki/DeWitt_Clinton_(locomotive) "DeWitt Clinton (locomotive)")*, built in 1831 for the [Mohawk and Hudson Railroad](/wiki/Mohawk_and_Hudson_Railroad "Mohawk and Hudson Railroad"), was a notable early locomotive.[[28]](#cite_note-Ellis,1968-29)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit), the original *John Bull* was on static display in the [National Museum of American History](/wiki/National_Museum_of_American_History "National Museum of American History") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[32]](#cite_note-33) The replica is preserved at the [Railroad Museum of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Railroad_Museum_of_Pennsylvania "Railroad Museum of Pennsylvania").[[33]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Adler_Originalfoto.jpg/250px-Adler_Originalfoto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adler_Originalfoto.jpg)

Photo of the *Adler* made in the early 1850s

In Germany, the first working steam locomotive was a rack-and-pinion engine, similar to the *Salamanca*, designed by the British locomotive pioneer [John Blenkinsop](/wiki/John_Blenkinsop "John Blenkinsop"). Built in June 1816 by [Johann Friedrich Krigar](/w/index.php?title=Johann_Friedrich_Krigar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Johann Friedrich Krigar (page does not exist)") in the Royal Berlin Iron Foundry (*Königliche Eisengießerei* zu Berlin), the locomotive ran on a circular track in the factory yard. It was the first locomotive to be built on the European mainland and the first steam-powered passenger service; curious onlookers could ride in the attached coaches for a fee. It is portrayed on a New Year's badge for the Royal Foundry dated 1816. Another locomotive was built using the same system in 1817. They were to be used on [pit railways](/wiki/Pit_railway "Pit railway") in [Königshütte](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsh%C3%BCtte_(Oberschlesien) "Königshütte (Oberschlesien)") and in Luisenthal on the Saar (today part of [Völklingen](/wiki/V%C3%B6lklingen "Völklingen")), but neither could be returned to working order after being dismantled, moved and reassembled. On 7 December 1835, the *[Adler](/wiki/Adler_(locomotive) "Adler (locomotive)")* ran for the first time between [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg") and [Fürth](/wiki/F%C3%BCrth "Fürth") on the [Bavarian Ludwig Railway](/wiki/Bavarian_Ludwig_Railway "Bavarian Ludwig Railway"). It was the 118th engine from the locomotive works of [Robert Stephenson](/wiki/Robert_Stephenson "Robert Stephenson") and stood under patent protection.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Cherepanov_steam_locomotive._drawing.jpg/250px-Cherepanov_steam_locomotive._drawing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cherepanov_steam_locomotive._drawing.jpg)

First locomotive in Russia. 1834

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), the [first steam locomotive](/wiki/Cherepanov_steam_locomotive "Cherepanov steam locomotive") was built in 1834 by [Cherepanovs](/wiki/Yefim_and_Miron_Cherepanov "Yefim and Miron Cherepanov"), however, it suffered from the lack of coal in the area and was replaced with horse traction after all the woods nearby had been cut down. The first Russian [Tsarskoye Selo steam railway](/wiki/Tsarskoye_Selo_Railway "Tsarskoye Selo Railway") started in 1837 with locomotives purchased from [Robert Stephenson and Company](/wiki/Robert_Stephenson_and_Company "Robert Stephenson and Company").

In 1837, the first steam railway started in Austria on the [Emperor Ferdinand Northern Railway](/wiki/Austrian_Northern_Railway "Austrian Northern Railway") between [Vienna-Floridsdorf](/wiki/Floridsdorf "Floridsdorf") and [Deutsch-Wagram](/wiki/Deutsch-Wagram "Deutsch-Wagram"). The oldest continually working steam engine in the world also runs in Austria: the [GKB 671](/wiki/S%C3%BCdbahn_Class_23_(old) "Südbahn Class 23 (old)") built in 1860, has never been taken out of service, and is still used for special excursions.

In 1838, the third steam locomotive to be built in Germany, the *[Saxonia](/wiki/Saxonia_(locomotive) "Saxonia (locomotive)")*, was manufactured by the *Maschinenbaufirma Übigau* near [Dresden](/wiki/Dresden "Dresden"), built by Prof. [Johann Andreas Schubert](/wiki/Johann_Andreas_Schubert "Johann Andreas Schubert"). The first independently designed locomotive in Germany was the *[Beuth](/wiki/Beuth_(locomotive) "Beuth (locomotive)")*, built by [August Borsig](/wiki/August_Borsig "August Borsig") in 1841. The first locomotive produced by [Henschel-Werke](/wiki/Henschel-Werke "Henschel-Werke") in [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), the *Drache*, was delivered in 1848.

The first steam locomotives operating in Italy were the *Bayard* and the *Vesuvio*, running on the [Napoli-Portici](/wiki/Napoli-Portici "Napoli-Portici") line, in the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies.

The first railway line over Swiss territory was the [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg")–[Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") line opened in 1844. Three years later, in 1847, the first fully Swiss railway line, the [*Spanisch Brötli Bahn*](/wiki/Swiss_Northern_Railway "Swiss Northern Railway"), from Zürich to Baden was opened.

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Australia")]

The arid nature of south Australia posed distinctive challenges to their early steam locomotion network. The high concentration of [magnesium chloride](/wiki/Magnesium_chloride "Magnesium chloride") in the well water ([bore water](/wiki/Water_well#Drilled_wells "Water well")) used in locomotive boilers on the [Trans-Australian Railway](/wiki/Trans-Australian_Railway "Trans-Australian Railway") caused serious and expensive maintenance problems. At no point along its route does the line cross a permanent freshwater watercourse, so bore water had to be relied on. No inexpensive treatment for the highly mineralised water was available, and locomotive boilers were lasting less than a quarter of the time normally expected.[[34]](#cite_note-35) In the days of steam locomotion, about half the total train load was water for the engine. The line's operator, [Commonwealth Railways](/wiki/Commonwealth_Railways "Commonwealth Railways"), was an early adopter of the [diesel-electric locomotive](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive#Diesel-electric "Diesel locomotive").

### Boiler

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Boiler")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Steam_locomotive "Special:EditPage/Steam locomotive") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(April 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The [fire-tube boiler](/wiki/Fire-tube_boiler "Fire-tube boiler") was standard practice for steam locomotive.[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Although other types of [boiler](/wiki/Boiler "Boiler") were evaluated they were not widely used, except for some 1,000 locomotives in Hungary which used the water-tube [Brotan boiler](/wiki/Brotan_boiler "Brotan boiler").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Cutaway_steam_locomotive.jpg/250px-Cutaway_steam_locomotive.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cutaway_steam_locomotive.jpg)

A steam locomotive with the boiler and firebox exposed (firebox on the left)

A boiler consists of a firebox where the fuel is burned, a barrel where water is turned into steam, and a smokebox which is kept at a slightly lower pressure than outside the firebox.

Solid fuel, such as wood, coal or coke, is either thrown into the firebox through a door by a [fireman](/wiki/Fireman_(steam_engine) "Fireman (steam engine)"), onto a set of [grates](/wiki/Glossary_of_steam_locomotive_components "Glossary of steam locomotive components") which hold the fuel in a bed as it burns, or in some cases, introduced to the firebox via a mechanical stoker. Ash falls through the grate into an ashpan. If oil is used as the fuel, a door is needed for adjusting the air flow, maintaining the firebox, and cleaning the oil jets.

The fire-tube boiler has internal tubes connecting the firebox to the smokebox through which the combustion gases flow transferring heat to the water. All the tubes together provide a large contact area, called the tube heating surface, between the gas and water in the boiler. Boiler water surrounds the firebox to stop the metal from becoming too hot. This is another area where the gas transfers heat to the water and is called the firebox heating surface. Ash and char collect in the smokebox as the gas gets drawn up the chimney (*stack* or *smokestack* in the US) by the exhaust steam from the cylinders.

The pressure in the boiler has to be monitored using a [steam gauge](/w/index.php?title=Steam_gauge&action=edit&redlink=1 "Steam gauge (page does not exist)") mounted in the cab. Steam pressure can be released manually by the driver or fireman. If the pressure reaches the boiler's design working limit, a [safety valve](/wiki/Safety_valve "Safety valve") opens automatically to reduce the pressure[[35]](#cite_note-36) and avoid a catastrophic accident.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Boiler_explosion_1850.jpg/250px-Boiler_explosion_1850.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boiler_explosion_1850.jpg)

Aftermath of a boiler explosion on a railway locomotive, c.1850

The exhaust steam from the engine cylinders shoots out of a nozzle pointing up the chimney in the smokebox. The steam entrains or drags the smokebox gases with it which maintains a lower pressure in the smokebox than that under the firebox grate. This pressure difference causes air to flow up through the coal bed and keeps the fire burning.

The search for [thermal efficiency](/wiki/Thermal_efficiency "Thermal efficiency") greater than that of a typical fire-tube boiler led engineers, such as [Nigel Gresley](/wiki/Nigel_Gresley "Nigel Gresley"), to consider the [water-tube boiler](/wiki/Water-tube_boiler "Water-tube boiler"). Although he tested the concept on the [LNER Class W1](/wiki/LNER_Class_W1 "LNER Class W1"), the difficulties during development exceeded the will to increase efficiency by that route.

The steam generated in the boiler not only moves the locomotive, but is also used to operate other devices such as the whistle, the air compressor for the brakes, the pump for replenishing the water in the boiler and the passenger car heating system. The constant demand for steam requires a periodic addition of further water in the boiler. The water is kept in a tank in the locomotive tender or wrapped around the boiler in the case of a [tank locomotive](/wiki/Tank_locomotive "Tank locomotive"). Periodic stops are required to refill the tanks; an alternative was a scoop installed under the tender that collected water as the train passed over a [track pan](/wiki/Track_pan "Track pan") located between the rails.

While the locomotive is producing steam, the amount of water in the boiler is constantly monitored by looking at the water level in a transparent tube, or sight glass. Efficient and safe operation of the boiler requires keeping the level in between lines marked on the sight glass. If the water level is too high, steam production falls, efficiency is lost and water is carried out with the steam into the cylinders, possibly causing mechanical damage. More seriously, if the water level gets too low, the crown sheet (top sheet) of the firebox becomes exposed. Without water on top of the sheet to transfer away the [heat of combustion](/wiki/Heat_of_combustion "Heat of combustion"), it softens and fails, letting high-pressure steam into the firebox and the cab. The development of the [fusible plug](/wiki/Fusible_plug "Fusible plug"), a temperature-sensitive device, ensured a controlled venting of steam into the firebox to warn the fireman to add water.

Scale builds up in the boiler and prevents adequate heat transfer, and corrosion eventually degrades the boiler materials to the point where it needs to be rebuilt or replaced. Start-up on a large engine may take hours of preliminary heating of the boiler water before sufficient steam is available.

Although the boiler is typically placed horizontally, for locomotives designed to work in locations with steep slopes it may be more appropriate to consider a [vertical boiler](/wiki/Vertical_boiler "Vertical boiler") or one mounted such that the boiler remains horizontal but the wheels are inclined to suit the slope of the rails.

### Steam circuit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Steam circuit")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Steam_locomotive "Special:EditPage/Steam locomotive") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(April 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/ParowozIR.jpg/250px-ParowozIR.jpg)](/wiki/File:ParowozIR.jpg)

[Thermal image](/wiki/Thermography "Thermography") of an operating steam locomotive

The steam generated in the boiler fills the space above the water in the partially filled boiler. Its maximum working pressure is limited by spring-loaded safety valves. It is then collected either in a perforated tube fitted above the water level or by a dome that often houses the regulator valve, or throttle, the purpose of which is to control the amount of steam leaving the boiler. The steam then either travels directly along and down a steam pipe to the engine unit or may first pass into the wet header of a [superheater](/wiki/Superheater "Superheater"), the role of the latter being to improve thermal efficiency and eliminate water droplets suspended in the "saturated steam", the state in which it leaves the boiler. On leaving the superheater, the steam exits the dry header of the superheater and passes down a steam pipe, entering the steam chests adjacent to the cylinders of a reciprocating engine. Inside each steam chest is a sliding valve that distributes the steam via ports that connect the steam chest to the ends of the cylinder space. The role of the valves is twofold: admission of each fresh dose of steam, and exhaust of the used steam once it has done its work.

The cylinders are double-acting, with steam admitted to each side of the piston in turn. In a two-cylinder locomotive, one cylinder is located on each side of the vehicle. The cranks are set 90° out of phase. During a full rotation of the driving wheel, steam provides four power strokes; each cylinder receives two injections of steam per revolution. The first stroke is to the front of the piston and the second stroke to the rear of the piston; hence two working strokes. Consequently, two deliveries of steam onto each piston face in the two cylinders generates a full revolution of the driving wheel. Each piston is attached to the driving axle on each side by a connecting rod, and the driving wheels are connected together by [coupling rods](/wiki/Coupling_rod "Coupling rod") to transmit power from the main driver to the other wheels. Note that at the two "[dead centres](/wiki/Dead_centres "Dead centres")", when the connecting rod is on the same axis as the crankpin on the driving wheel, the connecting rod applies no [torque](/wiki/Torque "Torque") to the wheel. The 90° degree phase difference ensures this doesn't happen to both at the same time.

Each [piston](/wiki/Piston "Piston") transmits power through a [crosshead](/wiki/Crosshead "Crosshead"), [connecting rod](/wiki/Connecting_rod "Connecting rod") (*Main rod* in the US) and a crankpin on the [driving wheel](/wiki/Driving_wheel "Driving wheel") (*Main driver* in the US) or to a [crank](/wiki/Crank_(mechanism) "Crank (mechanism)") on a driving axle. The movement of the valves in the steam chest is controlled through a set of rods and linkages called the [valve gear](/wiki/Valve_gear "Valve gear"), actuated from the driving axle or from the crankpin; the valve gear includes devices that allow reversing the engine, adjusting valve travel and the timing of the admission and exhaust events. The [cut-off](/wiki/Cutoff_(steam_engine) "Cutoff (steam engine)") point determines the moment when the valve blocks a steam port, "cutting off" admission steam and thus determining the proportion of the stroke during which steam is admitted into the cylinder; for example a 50% cut-off admits steam for half the stroke of the piston. The remainder of the stroke is driven by the expansive force of the steam. Careful use of cut-off provides economical use of steam and in turn, reduces fuel and water consumption. The reversing lever (*[Johnson bar](/wiki/Johnson_bar_(vehicle) "Johnson bar (vehicle)")* in the US), or screw-reverser (if so equipped), that controls the cut-off, therefore, performs a similar function to a [gearshift](/wiki/Gearshift "Gearshift") in an automobile – maximum cut-off, providing maximum [tractive effort](/wiki/Tractive_effort "Tractive effort") at the expense of efficiency, is used to pull away from a standing start, whilst a cut-off as low as 10% is used when cruising, providing reduced tractive effort, and therefore lower fuel/water consumption.[[36]](#cite_note-Ahrons,_1925-37)

Exhaust steam is directed upwards out of the locomotive through the chimney, by way of a nozzle called a [blastpipe](/wiki/Blastpipe "Blastpipe"), creating the familiar "chuffing" sound of the steam locomotive. The blastpipe is placed at a strategic point inside the smokebox that is at the same time traversed by the combustion gases drawn through the boiler and grate by the action of the steam blast. The combining of the two streams, steam and exhaust gases, is crucial to the efficiency of any steam locomotive, and the internal profiles of the chimney (or, strictly speaking, the *ejector*) require careful design and adjustment. This has been the object of intensive studies by a number of engineers (and often ignored by others, sometimes with catastrophic consequences). The fact that the draught depends on the exhaust pressure means that power delivery and power generation are automatically self-adjusting. Among other things, a balance has to be struck between obtaining sufficient draught for combustion whilst giving the exhaust gases and particles sufficient time to be consumed. In the past, a strong draught could lift the fire off the grate, or cause the ejection of unburnt particles of fuel, dirt and pollution for which steam locomotives had an unenviable reputation. Moreover, the pumping action of the exhaust has the counter-effect of exerting *back pressure* on the side of the piston receiving steam, thus slightly reducing cylinder power. Designing the exhaust ejector became a specific science, with engineers such as [Chapelon](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Chapelon "André Chapelon"), [Giesl](/wiki/Adolph_Giesl-Gieslingen "Adolph Giesl-Gieslingen") and [Porta](/wiki/Livio_Dante_Porta "Livio Dante Porta") making large improvements in thermal efficiency and a significant reduction in maintenance time[[37]](#cite_note-A1-38) and pollution.[[38]](#cite_note-39) A similar system was used by some early gasoline/kerosene [tractor](/wiki/Tractor "Tractor") manufacturers ([Advance-Rumely](/wiki/Advance-Rumely "Advance-Rumely")/[Hart-Parr](/wiki/Hart-Parr "Hart-Parr")) – the exhaust gas volume was vented through a cooling tower, allowing the steam exhaust to draw more air past the radiator.

Running gear animation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Washing_loco.jpg/250px-Washing_loco.jpg)](/wiki/File:Washing_loco.jpg)
