Note the [Belpaire](/wiki/Belpaire_firebox "Belpaire firebox") (square-topped) firebox.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/LNER_Class_A1_4-6-2_No60163_%27Tornado%27_%2829903372180%29.jpg/250px-LNER_Class_A1_4-6-2_No60163_%27Tornado%27_%2829903372180%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:LNER_Class_A1_4-6-2_No60163_%27Tornado%27_(29903372180).jpg)

[60163 *Tornado*](/wiki/LNER_Peppercorn_Class_A1_60163_Tornado "LNER Peppercorn Class A1 60163 Tornado") on the [East Coast Main Line](/wiki/East_Coast_Main_Line "East Coast Main Line") in 2016

Before the [1923 Grouping Act](/wiki/Railway_Act_1921 "Railway Act 1921"), production in the UK was mixed. The larger railway companies built locomotives in their own workshops, with the smaller ones and industrial concerns ordering them from outside builders. A large market for outside builders existed due to the home-build policy exercised by the main railway companies. An example of a pre-grouping works was the one at [Melton Constable](/wiki/Melton_Constable "Melton Constable"), which maintained and built some of the locomotives for the [Midland and Great Northern Joint Railway](/wiki/Midland_and_Great_Northern_Joint_Railway "Midland and Great Northern Joint Railway"). Other works included one at Boston (an early GNR building) and [Horwich Works](/wiki/Horwich_Works "Horwich Works").

Between 1923 and 1947, the Big Four railway companies (the Great Western Railway, the London, Midland & Scottish Railway, the [London & North Eastern Railway](/wiki/London_%26_North_Eastern_Railway "London & North Eastern Railway") and the Southern Railway) all built most of their own locomotives, only buying locomotives from outside builders when their own works were fully occupied (or as a result of government-mandated standardisation during wartime).[[88]](#cite_note-89)

From 1948, [British Railways](/wiki/British_Rail "British Rail") (BR) allowed the former Big Four companies (now designated as "Regions") to continue to produce their own designs, but also created a range of [standard](/wiki/Steam_locomotives_of_British_Railways#BR_standard_classes "Steam locomotives of British Railways") locomotives which supposedly combined the best features from each region. Although a policy of dieselisation was adopted in 1955, BR continued to build new steam locomotives until 1960, with the final engine being named *[Evening Star](/wiki/BR_Standard_Class_9F_92220_Evening_Star "BR Standard Class 9F 92220 Evening Star")*.[[89]](#cite_note-RG230360-90)

Some independent manufacturers produced steam locomotives for a few more years, with the last British-built industrial steam locomotive being constructed by [Hunslet](/wiki/Hunslet_Engine_Company "Hunslet Engine Company") in 1971. Since then, a few specialised manufacturers have continued to produce small locomotives for narrow gauge and miniature railways, but as the prime market for these is the tourist and [heritage railway](/wiki/Heritage_railway "Heritage railway") sector, the demand for such locomotives is limited. In November 2008, a new build main line steam locomotive, [60163 *Tornado*](/wiki/LNER_Peppercorn_Class_A1_60163_Tornado "LNER Peppercorn Class A1 60163 Tornado"), was tested on UK mainlines for eventual charter and tour use.

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Sweden")]

In the 19th and early 20th centuries, most Swedish steam locomotives were manufactured in Britain. Later, however, most steam locomotives were built by local factories including NOHAB in [Trollhättan](/wiki/Trollh%C3%A4ttan "Trollhättan") and ASJ in [Falun](/wiki/Falun "Falun"). One of the most successful types was the class "B" ([4-6-0](/wiki/4-6-0 "4-6-0")), inspired by the Prussian class P8. Many of the Swedish steam locomotives were preserved during the Cold War in case of war. During the 1990s, these steam locomotives were sold to non-profit associations or abroad, which is why the Swedish class B, class S ([2-6-4](/wiki/2-6-4 "2-6-4")) and class E2 ([2-8-0](/wiki/2-8-0 "2-8-0")) locomotives can now be seen in Britain, the Netherlands, Germany and Canada.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/California_Western_Railroad_Locomotive_45.jpg/250px-California_Western_Railroad_Locomotive_45.jpg)](/wiki/File:California_Western_Railroad_Locomotive_45.jpg)

[California Western Railroad No. 45](/wiki/California_Western_45 "California Western 45") (builder No. 58045), built by Baldwin in 1924, is a [2-8-2](/wiki/2-8-2 "2-8-2") *Mikado* locomotive. It is still in use today on the Skunk Train.

Locomotives for American railroads were nearly always built in the United States with very few imports, except in the earliest days of steam engines. This was due to the basic differences of markets in the United States which initially had many small markets located large distances apart, in contrast to Europe's higher density of markets. Locomotives that were cheap and rugged and could go large distances over cheaply built and maintained tracks were required. Once the manufacture of engines was established on a wide scale there was very little advantage to buying an engine from overseas that would have to be customised to fit the local requirements and track conditions. Improvements in engine design of both European and US origin were incorporated by manufacturers when they could be justified in a generally very conservative and slow-changing market. With the notable exception of the [USRA standard](/wiki/USRA_standard "USRA standard") locomotives built during World War I, in the United States, steam locomotive manufacture was always semi-customised. Railroads ordered locomotives tailored to their specific requirements, though some basic design features were always present. Railroads developed some specific characteristics; for example, the Pennsylvania Railroad and the [Great Northern Railway](/wiki/Great_Northern_Railway_(U.S.) "Great Northern Railway (U.S.)") had a preference for the Belpaire firebox.[[90]](#cite_note-91) In the United States, large-scale manufacturers constructed locomotives for nearly all rail companies, although nearly all major railroads had shops capable of heavy repairs and some railroads (for example, the [Norfolk and Western Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_and_Western_Railway "Norfolk and Western Railway") and the Pennsylvania Railroad, which had two erecting shops) constructed locomotives entirely in their own shops.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) Companies manufacturing locomotives in the US included [Baldwin Locomotive Works](/wiki/Baldwin_Locomotive_Works "Baldwin Locomotive Works"), [American Locomotive Company](/wiki/American_Locomotive_Company "American Locomotive Company") (ALCO), and [Lima Locomotive Works](/wiki/Lima_Locomotive_Works "Lima Locomotive Works"). Altogether, between 1830 and 1950, over 160,000 steam locomotives were built in the United States, with Baldwin accounting for the largest share, nearly 70,000.[[93]](#cite_note-Broggie2014pp25–26-94)

Steam locomotives required regular and, compared to a diesel-electric engine, frequent service and overhaul (often at government-regulated intervals in Europe and the US). Alterations and upgrades regularly occurred during overhauls. New appliances were added, unsatisfactory features removed, cylinders improved or replaced. Almost any part of the locomotive, including boilers, was replaced or upgraded. When service or upgrades got too expensive the locomotive was traded off or retired.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] On the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad two [2-10-2](/wiki/2-10-2 "2-10-2") locomotives were dismantled; the boilers were placed onto two new Class T [4-8-2](/wiki/4-8-2 "4-8-2") locomotives and the residual wheel machinery made into a pair of Class U [0-10-0](/wiki/0-10-0 "0-10-0") switchers with new boilers. [Union Pacific](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad")'s fleet of 3-cylinder [4-10-2](/wiki/4-10-2 "4-10-2") engines were converted into two-cylinder engines in 1942, because of high maintenance problems.

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Australia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/200th_steam_locomotive_built_by_Clyde_TF_1164_from_The_Powerhouse_Museum.jpg/250px-200th_steam_locomotive_built_by_Clyde_TF_1164_from_The_Powerhouse_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:200th_steam_locomotive_built_by_Clyde_TF_1164_from_The_Powerhouse_Museum.jpg)

The 200th steam locomotive built by [Clyde Engineering](/wiki/Clyde_Engineering "Clyde Engineering") (TF 1164) from the [Powerhouse Museum](/wiki/Powerhouse_Museum "Powerhouse Museum") collection

In [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Clyde Engineering](/wiki/Clyde_Engineering "Clyde Engineering") and the [Eveleigh Railway Workshops](/wiki/Eveleigh_Railway_Workshops "Eveleigh Railway Workshops") both built steam locomotives for the [New South Wales Government Railways](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Government_Railways "New South Wales Government Railways"). These include the [C38 class](/wiki/New_South_Wales_38_class_locomotive "New South Wales 38 class locomotive") [4-6-2](/wiki/4-6-2 "4-6-2"); the first five were built at Clyde with [streamlining](/wiki/Streamliner "Streamliner"), the other 25 locomotives were built at Eveleigh (13) and [Cardiff Workshops](/wiki/Cardiff_Locomotive_Workshops "Cardiff Locomotive Workshops") (12) near Newcastle. In Queensland, steam locomotives were locally constructed by [Walkers](/wiki/Walkers_Limited "Walkers Limited"). Similarly, the [South Australian Railways](/wiki/South_Australian_Railways "South Australian Railways") also manufactured steam locomotives locally at [Islington Railway Workshops](/wiki/Islington_Railway_Workshops "Islington Railway Workshops") in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"). [Victorian Railways](/wiki/Victorian_Railways "Victorian Railways") constructed most of their locomotives at its [Newport Workshops](/wiki/Newport_Workshops "Newport Workshops") and in [Bendigo](/wiki/Bendigo "Bendigo"), while in the early days locomotives were built at the [Phoenix Foundry](/wiki/Phoenix_Foundry "Phoenix Foundry") in [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat "Ballarat"). Locomotives constructed at the Newport shops ranged from the nA class [2-6-2](/wiki/2-6-2 "2-6-2")[T](/wiki/Side_tank_locomotive "Side tank locomotive") built for the [narrow gauge](/wiki/Narrow_gauge_lines_of_the_Victorian_Railways "Narrow gauge lines of the Victorian Railways"), up to the H class [4-8-4](/wiki/4-8-4 "4-8-4") – the largest conventional locomotive ever to operate in Australia, weighing 260 tons. However, the title of largest locomotive ever used in Australia goes to the 263-ton [New South Wales AD60 class locomotive](/wiki/New_South_Wales_AD60_class_locomotive "New South Wales AD60 class locomotive") [4-8-4+4-8-4](/wiki/4-8-4%2B4-8-4 "4-8-4+4-8-4") Garratt,[[94]](#cite_note-95) built by [Beyer, Peacock & Company](/wiki/Beyer,_Peacock_%26_Company "Beyer, Peacock & Company") in England. Most steam locomotives used in Western Australia were built in the United Kingdom, though some examples were designed and built locally at the [Western Australian Government Railways](/wiki/Western_Australian_Government_Railways "Western Australian Government Railways")' [Midland Railway Workshops](/wiki/Midland_Railway_Workshops "Midland Railway Workshops"). The 10 [WAGR S class](/wiki/WAGR_S_class "WAGR S class") locomotives (introduced in 1943) were the only class of steam locomotive to be wholly conceived, designed and built in Western Australia,[[95]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-96) while the Midland workshops notably participated in the Australia-wide construction program of [Australian Standard Garratts](/wiki/Australian_Standard_Garratt "Australian Standard Garratt") – these wartime locomotives were built at Midland in Western Australia, Clyde Engineering in New South Wales, Newport in Victoria and Islington in South Australia and saw varying degrees of service in all Australian states.[[95]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-96)

## The end of steam in general use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: The end of steam in general use")]

Main article: [Retirement of steam locomotives by country](/wiki/Retirement_of_steam_locomotives_by_country "Retirement of steam locomotives by country")

The introduction of [electric locomotives](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive") around the turn of the 20th century and later diesel-electric locomotives spelled the beginning of a decline in the use of steam locomotives, although it was some time before they were phased out of general use.[[96]](#cite_note-97) As diesel power (especially with electric transmission) became more reliable in the 1930s, it gained a foothold in North America.[[97]](#cite_note-Diesel,1935-98) The full transition away from steam power in North America took place during the 1950s. In continental Europe, large-scale electrification had replaced steam power by the 1970s. Steam was a familiar technology, adapted well to local facilities, and also consumed a wide variety of fuels; this led to its continued use in many countries until the end of the 20th century.

Steam engines have considerably less thermal efficiency than modern diesels, requiring constant maintenance and labour to keep them operational.[[98]](#cite_note-99) Water is required at many points throughout a rail network, making it a major problem in desert areas, as are found in some regions of the United States, Australia and South Africa. In places where water is available, it may be [hard](/wiki/Hard_water "Hard water"), which can cause "[scale](/wiki/Fouling "Fouling")" to form, composed mainly of [calcium carbonate](/wiki/Calcium_carbonate "Calcium carbonate"), [magnesium hydroxide](/wiki/Magnesium_hydroxide "Magnesium hydroxide") and [calcium sulfate](/wiki/Calcium_sulfate "Calcium sulfate"). Calcium and magnesium carbonates tend to be deposited as off-white solids on the inside the surfaces of pipes and [heat exchangers](/wiki/Heat_exchanger "Heat exchanger"). This precipitation is principally caused by thermal decomposition of [bicarbonate](/wiki/Bicarbonate "Bicarbonate") ions but also happens in cases where the [carbonate](/wiki/Carbonate "Carbonate") ion is at saturation concentration.[[99]](#cite_note-100) The resulting build-up of scale restricts the flow of water in pipes. In boilers, the deposits impair the flow of heat into the water, reducing the heating efficiency and allowing the metal boiler components to overheat.

The reciprocating mechanism on the driving wheels of a two-cylinder single expansion steam locomotive tended to pound the rails (see [hammer blow](/wiki/Hammer_blow "Hammer blow")), thus requiring more [maintenance](/wiki/Maintenance_of_way "Maintenance of way"). Raising steam from coal took a matter of hours, and created serious pollution problems. Coal-burning locomotives required fire cleaning and ash removal between turns of duty.[[100]](#cite_note-101) Diesel or electric locomotives, by comparison, drew benefit from new custom-built servicing facilities. The smoke from steam locomotives was also deemed objectionable; the first electric and diesel locomotives were developed in response to smoke abatement requirements,[[101]](#cite_note-BTC,1962-102) although this did not take into account the high level of less-visible pollution in [diesel exhaust](/wiki/Diesel_exhaust "Diesel exhaust") smoke, especially when idling. In some countries, however, power for electric locomotives is derived from steam generated in power stations, which are often run by coal.

Dramatic increases in the cost of diesel fuel prompted several initiatives to revive steam power.[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104) However, none of these has progressed to the point of production and, as of the early 21st century, steam locomotives operate only in a few isolated regions of the world and in tourist operations.

As early as 1975, railway enthusiasts in the United Kingdom began building new steam locomotives. That year, Trevor Barber completed his 2 ft (610 mm) gauge locomotive *Trixie* which ran on the [Meirion Mill Railway](/wiki/Meirion_Mill_Railway "Meirion Mill Railway").[[104]](#cite_note-105) From the 1990s onwards, the number of new builds being completed rose dramatically with new locos completed by the narrow-gauge [Ffestiniog](/wiki/Ffestiniog_Railway "Ffestiniog Railway") and [Corris](/wiki/Corris_Railway "Corris Railway") railways in Wales. The Hunslet Engine Company was revived in 2005, and began building steam locomotives on a commercial basis.[[105]](#cite_note-106) A standard-gauge [LNER Peppercorn Pacific](/wiki/LNER_Peppercorn_Class_A1 "LNER Peppercorn Class A1") "Tornado" was completed at [Hopetown Works](/wiki/Hopetown_Carriage_Works "Hopetown Carriage Works"), [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington"), and made its first run on 1 August 2008.[[106]](#cite_note-107)[[107]](#cite_note-108) It entered main line service later in 2008.[[108]](#cite_note-109) As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit) over half-a-dozen projects to build working replicas of extinct steam engines are going ahead, in many cases using existing parts from other types to build them. Examples include [BR 72010 *Hengist*](/wiki/BR_Standard_Class_6_72010_Hengist "BR Standard Class 6 72010 Hengist"),[[109]](#cite_note-110) BR Class 3MT No. 82045, BR Class 2MT No. 84030,[[110]](#cite_note-111) Brighton Atlantic *Beachy Head*,[[111]](#cite_note-112) the [LMS 5551 *The Unknown Warrior*](/wiki/LMS_Patriot_Class_5551_The_Unknown_Warrior "LMS Patriot Class 5551 The Unknown Warrior") project, GWR "[47xx](/wiki/GWR_4700_Class "GWR 4700 Class") 4709, [2999 *Lady of Legend*](/wiki/GWR_2900_Class_2999_Lady_of_Legend "GWR 2900 Class 2999 Lady of Legend"), [1014 *County of Glamorgan*](/wiki/GWR_1000_Class_1014_County_of_Glamorgan "GWR 1000 Class 1014 County of Glamorgan") and [6880 *Betton Grange*](/wiki/GWR_6800_Class_6880_Betton_Grange "GWR 6800 Class 6880 Betton Grange") projects. These United Kingdom based new build projects are further complemented by the new build [Pennsylvania Railroad 5550](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad_5550 "Pennsylvania Railroad 5550")[[112]](#cite_note-T1Trust01-113) project in the United States. One of the group's goals is to surpass the steam locomotive speed record held by [4468 *Mallard*](/wiki/LNER_Class_A4_4468_Mallard "LNER Class A4 4468 Mallard") when the 5550 is completed and for the 5550 to fill a gap in steam locomotive preservation.

In 1980, American financier [Ross Rowland](/wiki/Ross_Rowland "Ross Rowland") established American Coal Enterprises to develop a modernised coal-fired steam locomotive. His ACE 3000 concept attracted considerable attention, but was never built.[[113]](#cite_note-114)[[114]](#cite_note-115)

In 1998, in his book *The Red Devil and Other Tales from the Age of Steam*,[[115]](#cite_note-116) David Wardale put forward the concept of a high-speed high-efficiency "Super Class 5 4-6-0" locomotive for future steam haulage of tour trains on British main lines. The idea was formalised in 2001 by the formation of 5AT Project dedicated to developing and building the [5AT Advanced Technology Steam Locomotive](/wiki/5AT_Advanced_Technology_Steam_Locomotive "5AT Advanced Technology Steam Locomotive"), but it never received any major railway backing.

Locations where new builds are taking place include:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [GWR 1014 *County of Glamorgan*](/wiki/GWR_1000_Class_1014_County_of_Glamorgan "GWR 1000 Class 1014 County of Glamorgan") & [GWR 2999 *Lady of Legend*](/wiki/GWR_2900_Class_2999_Lady_of_Legend "GWR 2900 Class 2999 Lady of Legend"), both being built at [Didcot Railway Centre](/wiki/Didcot_Railway_Centre "Didcot Railway Centre")
* [GWR 6880 *Betton Grange*](/wiki/GWR_6800_Class_6880_Betton_Grange "GWR 6800 Class 6880 Betton Grange"), GWR 4709 & [LMS 5551 *The Unknown Warrior*](/wiki/LMS_Patriot_Class_5551_The_Unknown_Warrior "LMS Patriot Class 5551 The Unknown Warrior"), all being built at [Tyseley Locomotive Works](/wiki/Tyseley_Locomotive_Works "Tyseley Locomotive Works").[[116]](#cite_note-117)[[117]](#cite_note-118)
* [LNER 2007 *Prince of Wales*](/wiki/LNER_P2_Class_2007_Prince_of_Wales "LNER P2 Class 2007 Prince of Wales"), [Darlington Locomotive Works](/wiki/A1_Steam_Locomotive_Trust "A1 Steam Locomotive Trust")
* LNER 2001 Cock O' The North, Doncaster
* [Pennsylvania Railroad 5550](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad_5550 "Pennsylvania Railroad 5550"), [Pottstown, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pottstown,_Pennsylvania "Pottstown, Pennsylvania")[[112]](#cite_note-T1Trust01-113)
* [BR 72010 *Hengist*](/wiki/BR_Standard_Class_6_72010_Hengist "BR Standard Class 6 72010 Hengist"), [Great Central Railway](/wiki/Great_Central_Railway "Great Central Railway")
* BR 77021, TBA
* BR 82045, [Severn Valley Railway](/wiki/Severn_Valley_Railway "Severn Valley Railway")
* BR 84030 & LBSCR 32424 Beachy Head, both being built at [Bluebell Railway](/wiki/Bluebell_Railway "Bluebell Railway")
* MS&LR/GCR 567, Ruddington Great Central Railway, Northern Section
* VR V499, Victoria, Australia

In 2012, the [Coalition for Sustainable Rail](/w/index.php?title=Coalition_for_Sustainable_Rail&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coalition for Sustainable Rail (page does not exist)")[[118]](#cite_note-119) project was started in the US with the goal of creating a modern higher-speed steam locomotive, incorporating the improvements proposed by Livio Dante Porta and others, and using [torrefied biomass](/wiki/Torrefaction "Torrefaction") as solid fuel. The fuel has been recently developed by the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") in a collaboration between the university's [Institute on the Environment](/w/index.php?title=Institute_on_the_Environment&action=edit&redlink=1 "Institute on the Environment (page does not exist)") (IonE) and [Sustainable Rail International](/w/index.php?title=Sustainable_Rail_International&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sustainable Rail International (page does not exist)") (SRI), an organisation set up to explore the use of steam traction in a modern railway setup. The group have received the last surviving (but non-running) [ATSF 3460 class](/wiki/ATSF_3460_class "ATSF 3460 class") steam locomotive (No. 3463) via donation from its previous owner in Kansas, the Great Overland Station Museum. They hope to use it as a platform for developing "the world's cleanest, most powerful passenger locomotive", capable of speeds up to 130 mph (210 km/h). Named "Project 130", it aims to break the world steam-train speed record set by LNER Class A4 4468 Mallard in the UK at 126 mph (203 km/h). However, any demonstration of the project's claims is yet to be seen.

In Germany, a small number of fireless steam locomotives are still working in industrial service, e.g. at power stations, where an on-site supply of steam is readily available.

The small town of [Wolsztyn](/wiki/Wolsztyn "Wolsztyn"), Poland, approximately 60 kilometres (37 miles) from the historic city of [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań"), is the last place in the world where one can ride a regularly scheduled passenger train pulled by steam power. The locomotive shed at Wolsztyn is the last of its kind in the world. There are several working locomotives that haul daily commuter service between Wolsztyn, Poznan, Leszo and other neighboring cities. One can partake in footplate courses via The Wolsztyn Experience. There is no place left in the world that still operates daily, non-tourist steam powered commuter/passenger service other than here at Wolsztyn. There are several Polish-built OL49-class 2-6-2 general purpose locomotives and one PT47 class 2-8-2 in regular service. Each May, Wolsztyn is the site of a steam locomotive festival which brings visiting locomotives - often well over a dozen each year all operating. These operations are not done for tourism or museum/historical purposes; this is the last non-diesel rail line on the PKP (Polish State Network) that has been converted to diesel power.

The Swiss company Dampflokomotiv- und Maschinenfabrik [DLM AG](/wiki/DLM_AG "DLM AG") delivered eight steam locomotives to rack railways in Switzerland and Austria between 1992 and 1996. Four of them are now the main traction on the [Brienz Rothorn Bahn](/wiki/Brienz_Rothorn_Bahn "Brienz Rothorn Bahn"); the four others were built for the [Schafbergbahn](/wiki/Schafbergbahn "Schafbergbahn") in Austria, where they run 90% of the trains.

The same company also rebuilt a German [DR Class 52.80](/wiki/DR_Class_52.80 "DR Class 52.80") 2-10-0 locomotive to new standards with modifications such as roller bearings, light oil firing and boiler insulation.[[119]](#cite_note-120)

## Environmental impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Environmental impact")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w.svg) | This section **is missing information** about non-CO2 [air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution"). Please expand the section to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Steam_locomotive "Talk:Steam locomotive"). *(October 2025)* |

Steam locomotives output [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") as a byproduct of their normal operation, this has a negative impact on the climate, which may place steam locomotives in violation of government legislation with the intention of reducing [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). The future use of steam locomotives is therefore in doubt. For example, in the United Kingdom, the government has passed legislation which prohibits the mining of coal in the UK. This is causing the price of coal in the country to increase, as it must be imported before use, therefore increasing the cost of operating coal-fired steam locomotives in the country. This directly negates from the profits of heritage railways across the country, which must account for the extra cost in their operations.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

In the US, many tourist railroads use oil-fired steam locomotives (or have converted their locomotives to run on oil) to reduce their environmental footprint, and because [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") can be easier to obtain than coal of the proper type and sizing for locomotives. For example, the [Grand Canyon Railway](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_Railway "Grand Canyon Railway") runs its steam locomotives on used vegetable oil.

An organization called the Coalition for Sustainable Rail (CSR) is developing an environmentally friendly coal substitute made from [torrefied](/wiki/Torrefied "Torrefied") [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass").[[121]](#cite_note-csrail.org-122) In early 2019, they performed a series of tests using [Everett Railroad](/wiki/Everett_Railroad "Everett Railroad") to evaluate the performance of the [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel"), with positive results. The biofuel was found to burn slightly faster and hotter than coal.[[122]](#cite_note-123) The goal of the project is primarily to find a sustainable fuel for historic steam locomotives on tourist railroads, but CSR has also suggested that, in the future, steam locomotives powered by torrefied biomass could be an environmentally and economically superior alternative to diesel locomotives.[[121]](#cite_note-csrail.org-122) Also, a large vat containing salt may be used without needing to replenishing the medium. Large heating elements would be one method of recharging the system, however, it is possible to pump molten salt as well, removing the cooled salt and replenishing from facilities which contain a much larger vat.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Steam locomotives in popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Steam_locomotive&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Steam locomotives in popular culture")]

Steam locomotives have been present in popular culture since the 19th century. Folk songs from that period including "[I've Been Working on the Railroad](/wiki/I%27ve_Been_Working_on_the_Railroad "I've Been Working on the Railroad")" and the "[Ballad of John Henry](/wiki/John_Henry_(folklore) "John Henry (folklore)")" are a mainstay of American music and culture.

Many steam locomotive toys have been made, and [railway modelling](/wiki/Rail_transport_modelling "Rail transport modelling") is a popular hobby.

Steam locomotives are often portrayed in fictional works, notably *[The Railway Series](/wiki/The_Railway_Series "The Railway Series")* by the [Rev W. V. Awdry](/wiki/Wilbert_Awdry "Wilbert Awdry"), *[The Little Engine That Could](/wiki/The_Little_Engine_That_Could "The Little Engine That Could")* by [Watty Piper](/wiki/Watty_Piper "Watty Piper"), *[Starlight Express](/wiki/Starlight_Express "Starlight Express")* by [Andrew Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Andrew_Lloyd_Webber "Andrew Lloyd Webber"), *[The Polar Express](/wiki/The_Polar_Express "The Polar Express")* by [Chris Van Allsburg](/wiki/Chris_Van_Allsburg "Chris Van Allsburg"), and the [Hogwarts Express](/wiki/Hogwarts_Express "Hogwarts Express") from [J.K. Rowling](/wiki/J.K._Rowling "J.K. Rowling")'s Harry Potter series. They have also been featured in many children's television shows, such as *[Thomas & Friends](/wiki/Thomas_%26_Friends "Thomas & Friends")*, based on characters from the books by Awdry, and *[Ivor the Engine](/wiki/Ivor_the_Engine "Ivor the Engine")* created by [Oliver Postgate](/wiki/Oliver_Postgate "Oliver Postgate").

The Hogwarts Express also appears in the Harry Potter series of films, portrayed by [GWR 4900 Class 5972 Olton Hall](/wiki/GWR_4900_Class_5972_Olton_Hall "GWR 4900 Class 5972 Olton Hall") in a special Hogwarts livery. The Polar Express appears in the [animated movie of the same name](/wiki/The_Polar_Express_(film) "The Polar Express (film)").

An elaborate, themed [funicular](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular") [Hogwarts Express ride](/wiki/Hogwarts_Express_(Universal_Orlando_Resort) "Hogwarts Express (Universal Orlando Resort)") is featured in the [Universal Orlando Resort](/wiki/Universal_Orlando_Resort "Universal Orlando Resort") in Florida, connecting the Harry Potter section of Universal Studios with the Islands of Adventure theme park.

The Polar Express is recreated on many heritage railroads in the United States, including the North Pole Express pulled by the [Pere Marquette 1225](/wiki/Pere_Marquette_1225 "Pere Marquette 1225") locomotive, which is operated by the [Steam Railroading Institute](/wiki/Steam_Railroading_Institute "Steam Railroading Institute") in [Owosso, Michigan](/wiki/Owosso,_Michigan "Owosso, Michigan"). According to author Van Allsburg, this locomotive was the inspiration for the story and it was used in the production of the movie.

A number of computer and video games feature steam locomotives, such as Railroad Tycoon and [Railway Empire](/wiki/Railway_Empire "Railway Empire"). *[Railroad Tycoon](/wiki/Sid_Meier%27s_Railroad_Tycoon "Sid Meier's Railroad Tycoon")*, produced in 1990, was named "one of the best computer games of the year".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are two notable examples of steam locomotives used as [charges](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry) "Charge (heraldry)") on heraldic [coats of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms"). One is that of [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington"), which displays *[Locomotion No. 1](/wiki/Locomotion_No._1 "Locomotion No. 1")*. The other is the original coat of arms of [Swindon](/wiki/Swindon "Swindon"), not currently in use, which displays a basic steam locomotive.[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/2007_UT_Proof.png/120px-2007_UT_Proof.png)](/wiki/File:2007_UT_Proof.png)

The [state quarter](/wiki/50_State_Quarters "50 State Quarters") representing Utah, depicting the [golden spike](/wiki/Golden_spike "Golden spike") ceremony

Steam locomotives are a popular topic for coin collectors.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The 1950 Silver 5 Peso coin of Mexico has a steam locomotive on its reverse as the prominent feature.

The 20 euro [Biedermeier Period coin](/wiki/Euro_gold_and_silver_commemorative_coins_(Austria)#2003_coinage "Euro gold and silver commemorative coins (Austria)"), minted 11 June 2003, shows on the obverse an early model steam locomotive (the *[Ajax](/wiki/KFNB_Minotaurus_and_Ajax "KFNB Minotaurus and Ajax")*) on Austria's first railway line, the [Kaiser Ferdinands-Nordbahn](/wiki/Emperor_Ferdinand_Northern_Railway "Emperor Ferdinand Northern Railway"). The *Ajax* can still be seen today in the [Technisches Museum Wien](/wiki/Technisches_Museum_Wien "Technisches Museum Wien").
As part of the [50 State Quarters](/wiki/50_State_Quarters "50 State Quarters") program, the quarter representing the US state of Utah depicts the ceremony where the two halves of the [First transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad") met at [Promontory Summit](/wiki/Promontory_Summit,_Utah "Promontory Summit, Utah") in 1869. The coin recreates a popular image from the ceremony with steam locomotives from each company facing each other while the [golden spike](/wiki/Golden_spike "Golden spike") is being driven.

The novel "[Night on the Galactic Railroad](/wiki/Night_on_the_Galactic_Railroad "Night on the Galactic Railroad")"[[125]](#cite_note-126) by [Kenji Miyazawa](/wiki/Kenji_Miyazawa "Kenji Miyazawa") is centered on the idea of a steam train traveling among the stars. Miyazawa's novel later inspired [Leiji Matsumoto](/wiki/Leiji_Matsumoto "Leiji Matsumoto")'s successful "[Galaxy Express 999](/wiki/Galaxy_Express_999 "Galaxy Express 999")" series.

Another Japanese televisual franchise, [Super Sentai](/wiki/Super_Sentai "Super Sentai"), features monsters based on steam locomotives.

Charge Man, a Robot Master from the fifth installment of the [Mega Man](/wiki/Mega_Man "Mega Man") series is based on a steam locomotive.
